---
title: Templates
---

::: {.callout-tip}
Click the `</>` button in the top navigation bar to view the original `.qmd` source code.
:::

Every rendered element---code blocks, figures, fenced divs, bracketed spans---passes through a **template** before becoming output. Templates are files with `{{variable}}` placeholders that control the markup for a single element.

Templates can be provided by plugins (in their `elements/` and `templates/` directories), placed in `_calepin/` (project), or `~/.config/calepin/` (user). *Calepin* checks in this order:

1. Plugin-provided directories (in plugin order)
2. `_calepin/elements/` or `_calepin/templates/` (project)
3. `~/.config/calepin/elements/` or `~/.config/calepin/templates/` (user)
4. Built-in (compiled into the binary)


# Element templates

A template is a file with `{{variable}}` placeholders. For example, the built-in `div.html`:

```html
<div class="{{classes}}"{{id-attr}}>
{{children}}
</div>
```

Any `{{variable}}` not defined is replaced with an empty string. Templates have no conditionals---all logic is computed in Rust and injected as pre-formatted variables.

## Naming and resolution

Templates are named `{element}.{format}` where format is `html`, `tex`, `typ`, or `md`:

- `callout-note.html` --- HTML template for `::: {.callout-note}`
- `theorem.tex` --- LaTeX template for `::: {.theorem}`
- `mystyle.html` --- HTML template for `[text]{.mystyle}`

Resolution order:

1. Plugin `elements/` directory (in plugin order)
2. `_calepin/elements/{name}.{ext}` (project override)
3. `~/.config/calepin/elements/{name}.{ext}` (user default)
4. Built-in (compiled into the binary)

A plugin can bundle template overrides by declaring `provides.elements.dir` in its `plugin.yml`. See [Plugins](plugins.qmd) for details.

## Div variables

Fenced divs (`::: {.classname #my-id key="value"} ... :::`) receive:

| Variable | Description |
|----------|-------------|
| `{{children}}` | Recursively rendered content |
| `{{classes}}` | Space-separated class list |
| `{{id}}` | Element id (empty if none) |
| `{{id-attr}}` | ` id="..."` attribute string (empty if none) |
| `{{label}}` | Format-specific label: `\label{id}` (LaTeX), `<id>` (Typst) |
| `{{number}}` | Auto-incremented counter (theorem environments only) |
| *user attrs* | Any `key="value"` pairs from the div header |

Template lookup tries each class in order, then falls back to `div.{ext}`.

## Span variables

Bracketed spans (`[text]{.myclass key="val"}`) receive:

| Variable | Description |
|----------|-------------|
| `{{content}}` | Text inside the brackets |
| `{{class}}` | First class |
| `{{classes}}` | Space-separated class list |
| `{{id}}` / `{{id-attr}}` | Same as divs |
| *user attrs* | Any key-value pairs |

If no template matches, *Calepin* falls back to `<span>` (HTML), `\text{}` (LaTeX), or `[]` (Typst).

## Code block variables

| Variable | Description |
|----------|-------------|
| `{{code}}` | Raw source code |
| `{{lang}}` | Language identifier |
| `{{label}}` | Chunk label |
| `{{highlighted}}` | Syntax-highlighted code |

Templates: `code-source`, `code-output` (`{{output}}`), `code-warning`, `code-message`, `code-error`.

## Figure variables

See [Figures](figures.qmd#figure-templates) for figure template variables.

## Custom template example

The file `_calepin/elements/highlight-box.html`:

```html
<div class="highlight-box" style="background: {{color}};
     border-left: 4px solid #333; padding: 0.8em 1em; margin: 1em 0;"{{id-attr}}>
<strong>{{title}}</strong>
{{children}}
</div>
```

Used as:

    ::: {.highlight-box title="Key takeaway" color="#e8f4f8"}
    Templates are the simplest way to customize *Calepin*.
    :::

::: {.highlight-box title="Key takeaway" color="#e8f4f8"}
Templates are the simplest way to customize *Calepin*. Drop a file in `_calepin/elements/` and any fenced div with that class will use it.
:::

::: {.highlight-box title="Different colors work too" color="#fef3c7"}
Each instance can pass different key-value attributes. Here `color` is yellow instead of blue.
:::


# Page templates

The page template wraps the entire rendered body in a document shell---the `<html>`, `<head>`, CSS, JavaScript, etc. To customize the page layout, drop a file in `_calepin/templates/`:

- `_calepin/templates/calepin.html` --- HTML page wrapper
- `_calepin/templates/calepin.css` --- default stylesheet
- `_calepin/templates/calepin.latex` --- LaTeX document wrapper
- `_calepin/templates/calepin.typ` --- Typst document wrapper

Same resolution: plugin → project → user (`~/.config/calepin/templates/`) → built-in.

## Page template variables

| Variable | Description |
|----------|-------------|
| `{{body}}` | Rendered document content |
| `{{plain-title}}` | Title as plain text |
| `{{title}}` / `{{author}}` / `{{date}}` | Metadata fields |
| `{{title-block}}` | Pre-rendered title markup |
| `{{subtitle-block}}` / `{{author-block}}` / `{{date-block}}` | Pre-rendered metadata blocks |
| `{{abstract-block}}` / `{{keywords-block}}` | Pre-rendered if present |
| `{{appendix-block}}` | License, citation, copyright, funding |
| `{{css}}` | Stylesheet tags (HTML) |
| `{{header-includes}}` | Raw content for `<head>` |
| `{{include-before}}` / `{{include-after}}` | Content before/after body |
| `{{preamble}}` | Extra preamble content |
| `{{js}}` | JavaScript block |

For example, to create a minimal HTML page:

```html
<!DOCTYPE html>
<html>
<head>
<meta charset="utf-8">
<title>{{plain-title}}</title>
{{css}}
</head>
<body>
<h1>{{title}}</h1>
{{body}}
</body>
</html>
```

Save as `_calepin/templates/calepin.html` and all HTML output in your project will use it. This is how you create blog layouts, slide shells, dashboard wrappers, or any other HTML variation without writing Rust.
